skills.name: Load-Bearing
skills.definition: Training in managing physical load across the body. This skill represents refined technique in carrying, hauling, and supporting heavy objects using efficient body posture and breathing control. It serves as the foundation for all advanced feats of strength involving static or mobile weight. Those trained in Load-Bearing are known for unshakable footing and calculated strength application under stress.

skills.name: Powerlifting
skills.definition: Focused on the exertion of maximum strength in short, controlled lifts. Powerlifting emphasizes raw force in foundational lift forms—often with explosive or peak-effort movement. While rooted in personal challenge, it’s frequently used to overcome immovable obstacles or demonstrate sheer might. This skill builds the base for more refined and specialized lifting techniques.

skills.name: Deadlift Mastery
skills.definition: The lifter becomes one with the weight—executing a clean, grounded lift with spine-aligned control. This skill reflects perfection in lifting objects directly from the ground, whether for raw strength display or practical heavy hauling. Practitioners can move dead weight with surprising balance and control, avoiding wasted energy or strain. It is the mark of one who knows how to rise with power.

skills.name: Max Squat
skills.definition: This skill represents the perfected ability to lower and raise heavy loads using leg and core synergy. Max Squat is used when bearing weight that must be braced, absorbed, and controlled before movement. It symbolizes discipline under strain—knowing how to descend without collapse and rise without panic. Those trained in this skill are anchors in moments when stability is everything.

skills.name: Clean and Press Pro
skills.definition: A hybrid of power and control, this skill reflects the lifter’s ability to raise weight in stages and finish with an overhead drive. Clean and Press Pro represents both technical discipline and explosive follow-through. Often used in practical lifting or symbolic acts of victory, it’s a controlled storm of motion. Those who master it carry the mark of precision strength.

skills.name: Endurance Carry
skills.definition: Where Powerlifting is short and explosive, Endurance Carry is slow and relentless. This skill is about bearing weight over time—across distance, terrain, or repeated use. Practitioners know how to pace the body, conserve energy, and lock into a rhythm that carries them far beyond raw strength alone. It’s not a sprint—it’s the will to bear the burden until the job is done.

skills.name: Long Haul March
skills.definition: The art of walking with weight without breaking stride. Long Haul March reflects discipline in posture, footwork, and load adjustment over long distances. It is often trained for migrations, patrols, or missions where there is no relief until the end is earned. The journey isn’t lighter—but you carry it like it is.

skills.name: Farmers Walk
skills.definition: A test of grip, stride, and spinal endurance, Farmers Walk trains the body to move under side-heavy or imbalanced load. This skill allows for controlled walking with weight in each hand—common in rural, industrial, or supply-based roles. It’s functional strength at its core, building patience and resilience through motion. When others drag, you walk like the weight belongs to you.

skills.name: Pack Shuffle
skills.definition: Designed for forward motion under awkward or rear-weighted loads, Pack Shuffle emphasizes adaptability and pace. It is used when carrying full loads across terrain too unstable or narrow for full strides. Practitioners learn how to pivot, rest mid-march, and shift weight subtly with each step. Not fast, not flashy—just unstoppable.

skills.name: Bulk Transfer
skills.definition: A team skill at heart, Bulk Transfer is the art of coordinating with others to move large or cumbersome objects. It focuses on timing, shared effort, and vocal or gesture-based signaling to maintain balance and prevent accidents. This skill is essential in labor, rescue, and construction scenarios where solo strength is not enough. It turns many into one—and weight into movement.

skills.name: Coordinated Lift Leader
skills.definition: This skill designates the one who commands rhythm and cues during a shared lift. A Coordinated Lift Leader ensures safety and unity across a group haul, adapting to shifting terrain or strain levels on the fly. It’s not just about lifting—it’s about directing energy where it's needed most. Those trained in this role carry both the weight and the team.

skills.name: Group Weight Rhythm
skills.definition: More than brute effort, this skill teaches attunement to group flow. Practitioners instinctively sync breathing, pace, and movement with others during a shared carry. It reduces shock, tilting, or drift—ensuring smoother motion for everyone involved. In a world where chaos breaks things, rhythm holds it all together.

skills.name: Shift Weight Control
skills.definition: This skill enables subtle redistribution of weight mid-motion—either to assist a struggling teammate or correct imbalance. Shift Weight Control is used in tight spaces, steep angles, or unstable settings where every pound must be placed with intent. It prevents disaster before it starts. The best practitioners make the hardest jobs look smooth.

skills.name: Burst Power
skills.definition: The ability to harness stored strength in a single, high-impact moment. Burst Power is used to deliver rapid, explosive force—whether breaking free from restraints, shoving obstacles, or initiating a powerful strike. It emphasizes controlled detonation of effort, not prolonged application. This skill is the foundation of all techniques where sudden strength must overwhelm resistance.

skills.name: Door Breach
skills.definition: Training focused on applying concentrated force to barriers, specifically doors, gates, or blockades. Door Breach combines timing, angle, and raw strength to create failure in hinges, locks, or latches. Often used in tactical or emergency situations, it allows entry without finesse—only sheer presence. The goal isn’t subtlety—it’s immediate access.

skills.name: Ram Charge
skills.definition: A full-body impact designed to displace or disable targets or barriers with brute force. Ram Charge reflects precision in converting speed and mass into a forward strike, often initiated from a short distance. It’s used when there’s no time to slow down and no room for negotiation. Those who master it move like a battering ram in human form.

skills.name: Hinge Pop
skills.definition: A focused application of pressure designed to disrupt a structural weak point. Hinge Pop is commonly used to remove or dislocate mounted barriers like doors, panels, or trap lids. The technique relies on knowing where to strike—and how to do so with minimum wasted effort. It’s quiet, fast, and devastating when used correctly.

skills.name: Jam Shear
skills.definition: This skill targets mechanical or structural jams, applying sharp, decisive bursts of strength to shear, snap, or force movement. Jam Shear is used on tools, locked items, stuck panels, or pressure points that resist normal movement. Practitioners know how to shift their force sideways or diagonally for maximum leverage. In short: when it’s stuck, you make it move.

skills.name: Explosive Sprint
skills.definition: A rapid, strength-driven dash meant to close short distances with overwhelming speed. Explosive Sprint isn’t about endurance—it’s about sudden propulsion. Useful for takedowns, charge openings, or escapes, this skill prioritizes raw thrust and leg drive over form. You don’t run with it—you launch.

skills.name: Start Dash
skills.definition: This skill perfects the moment when movement begins—the first step, the weight shift, the launch. Start Dash is trained for those who need the edge in reaction-based movement or combat openings. Whether from stillness or a crouch, it delivers instant motion when time is critical. It’s not how fast you run—it’s how fast you leave the ground.

skills.name: Shoulder Tackle
skills.definition: A kinetic strike using the shoulder to unbalance, topple, or displace a target. Shoulder Tackle converts linear momentum into close-range impact without needing a weapon. It’s used to break lines, burst through crowds, or slam opponents against surfaces. Practitioners are trained to absorb counter-force without losing balance.

skills.name: Launch Leap
skills.definition: A strength-based leap that emphasizes vertical or directional propulsion from a standing or moving position. Launch Leap is used to cross gaps, gain vertical advantage, or surprise targets with airborne aggression. This is not finesse—it’s the violent arc of controlled strength. Practiced users make jumps others won’t even attempt.

skills.name: Sledge Impact
skills.definition: Specialization in delivering full-body or tool-assisted strikes with maximum momentum. Sledge Impact is not about speed—it’s about mass meeting motion. This skill is used when breaking through dense material, battering armor, or demolishing cover. It teaches how to brace for recoil and direct force cleanly into a target.

skills.name: Over The Top Swing
skills.definition: A rotational strike or lift involving overhead momentum, often used with tools, beams, or improvised weapons. Over The Top Swing relies on coordinated timing of shoulders, spine, and arms to deliver crushing arcs. It’s used to breach cover, disable targets, or redistribute weight quickly. The swing doesn’t just land—it crashes.

skills.name: Rebound Control
skills.definition: The art of managing reactive force after a strike or impact. Rebound Control is trained to prevent stumbles, drops, or unintended movement following high-force action. It’s especially valuable in confined areas, group lifts, or when wielding heavy tools. Skilled users absorb shock like it was never there.

skills.name: Impact Follow Through
skills.definition: This skill extends momentum beyond initial contact—ensuring that strikes, lifts, or collisions finish with intent. Impact Follow Through prevents energy loss during critical moments, whether driving a ram, delivering a final blow, or throwing weight into a shove. It is not about stopping—it’s about pushing through the end of force.

skills.name: Material Deformation
skills.definition: This skill represents the application of strength to reshape, distort, or break solid objects through raw force. Material Deformation is the foundation for feats like bending metal, cracking stone, or crushing containers. Practitioners understand how to apply pressure efficiently to force objects beyond their natural limits. It's a blend of patience, technique, and overwhelming strength.

skills.name: Metal Bend
skills.definition: Focused on the distortion of rigid metallic objects using pure physical pressure. Metal Bend is used to warp bars, twist frames, or unfasten hardened pieces without tools. It requires constant pressure applied with precision and awareness of weak points. Those who master it can reshape what others see as permanent.

skills.name: Iron Rod Twist
skills.definition: A specialized expression of rotational power applied to long, resistant objects. Iron Rod Twist is used to coil, snap, or spiral rigid rods through torque and grip strength. It is most often performed as a display of control over cold steel or structural materials. To twist iron is to show that resistance is negotiable.

skills.name: Pry Bar Spread
skills.definition: This skill focuses on leveraging force outward to split or open firmly locked or sealed spaces. Pry Bar Spread simulates the effect of prying open reinforced objects using nothing but raw arm and shoulder pressure. Practitioners learn to push outward while bracing the body for counterforce. It’s not about finesse—it’s about cracking the uncrackable.

skills.name: Horshoe Crush
skills.definition: A feat of compacted grip strength and sheer will, this skill enables the crushing or folding of ring-shaped metals and similar structures. Horseshoe Crush is often trained for performance, intimidation, or demonstration of dominant strength. It's one of the clearest visual signals of total hand control under resistance.

skills.name: Stone Crack
skills.definition: Trained application of strength to brittle or dense surfaces with the intent to fracture. Stone Crack is used to test walls, break rocks, or disrupt ancient structures during excavation. It relies on consistent pressure and the ability to sense where the stress lines lie. Power meets patience in this calculated art.

skills.name: Chisel Strike
skills.definition: Chisel Strike channels controlled bursts of force through a blunt or sharpened edge, often used to begin separation or weaken structures. It's trained for repeated, targeted impacts where precision and rhythm are more important than raw speed. The wielder becomes the hammer—and the strike becomes the solution.

skills.name: Wedge Split
skills.definition: A skill in driving a wedge—or acting as one—through an object to force a split. Wedge Split combines angled pressure with patient, escalating force. Practitioners can drive through beams, bone, or barricade given enough leverage. It’s strength used not to lift, but to divide.

skills.name: Masonry Fracture
skills.definition: Mastery of breaking through stonework using repeated strikes or leveraged force. Masonry Fracture is used when precision demolition or breach work is needed without explosives or tools. The character becomes the force that makes the wall fall. It is physical tenacity shaped like a battering mantra.

skills.name: Press Crush
skills.definition: The art of compressing objects between two limbs or surfaces with intense, slow pressure. Press Crush is trained to crush containers, armor, or bone through unyielding closing force. It’s as much about control as it is about power—pressing without shaking, destroying without slipping.

skills.name: Container Crush
skills.definition: A refinement of Press Crush, this skill is focused on breaking enclosed objects such as chests, crates, or canisters. Container Crush is used when access is more important than preservation. The practitioner understands how to break through walls of resistance without needing tools. What’s sealed stays sealed—until you decide otherwise.

skills.name: Panel Buckle
skills.definition: Specialized in applying force across wide, thin surfaces to cause bending or failure. Panel Buckle is used on gates, vehicle siding, or structural supports where a ripple effect of collapse is required. The user learns how to direct weight, angle, and bracing to fold materials inward or outward.

skills.name: Vise Clamp
skills.definition: This skill represents the application of body or grip as a locking clamp to hold or crush objects between surfaces. Vise Clamp is used to immobilize, crush, or restrain items or enemies. It is often used in interrogation, restraint, or containment scenarios where escape is unacceptable. You don’t let go—because you are the vice.

skills.name: Projection
skills.definition: The art of transferring strength into external motion—throwing, hurling, or launching with force and intent. Projection reflects your ability to shift weight from the body into a moving object. It is the foundation for all physical ranged applications, from tossing tools to throwing enemies. A well-trained throw doesn’t just move—it makes impact matter.

skills.name: Distance Throw
skills.definition: This skill focuses on maximizing the range of thrown objects through perfect timing, explosive power, and release angle. Distance Throw is used when covering ground matters more than precision. From launching ropes to lobbing heavy objects, it’s about getting there fast and far. Your arm becomes a catapult.

skills.name: Hammer Toss
skills.definition: A rotational throw using core torque and shoulder control to launch a heavy object in a wide arc. Hammer Toss is ideal for weighted weapons, chained tools, or any object requiring spin to fly true. Practitioners understand how to build speed in motion before the final explosive release. The more it whirls, the farther it flies.

skills.name: Caber Heave
skills.definition: Based on ancient trials of might, this skill captures the upward-forward launch of tall or awkwardly shaped objects. Caber Heave prioritizes posture, grip shift, and explosive rise from the hips to control tumbling mass. It’s not just about throwing—it’s about launching chaos with intent.

skills.name: Overhead Fling
skills.definition: A direct vertical-to-arc throw initiated from above the shoulders. Overhead Fling is ideal when clearing walls, tossing onto roofs, or sending objects over obstacles. It requires stable footing and momentum control to prevent self-injury or loss of direction. Height becomes your weapon.

skills.name: Accuracy Throw
skills.definition: This skill hones the ability to hit specific targets or land objects precisely where needed. Accuracy Throw combines strength with line-of-sight calculation, object balance, and fingertip control. It’s trained for delivering payloads, triggering mechanisms, or striking enemies with certainty. Where you aim, it lands.

skills.name: Pinpoint Drop
skills.definition: A precision throw used for short-range delivery, often from elevation or into confined spaces. Pinpoint Drop allows careful placement of tools, packages, or weight without damage or alert. It blends patience and control—more archery than brute toss. The right hand, at the right angle, at the right time.

skills.name: Window Thread
skills.definition: A throw trained to slip an object through tight gaps—windows, holes, cages, or between guards. Window Thread relies on posture adjustment, object rotation, and velocity tapering. Practitioners can bypass defenses or deliver aid in ways no other motion allows. It’s finesse cloaked in strength.

skills.name: Center Mass Lob
skills.definition: A heavy-targeted throw focused on delivering impact at the core of a person or object. Center Mass Lob sacrifices spin and flash for reliable, thudding contact. It’s used to unbalance enemies, knock down constructs, or distract at close-to-mid range. You don’t aim for flair—you aim for the middle and make it hurt.

skills.name: Arc Control
skills.definition: Arc Control refines how objects travel after leaving the hand—predicting rise, fall, and curve. Practitioners train to hit moving targets or pass over defenses while adjusting for wind, spin, and weight. It is both science and instinct—throwing not just hard, but smart.

skills.name: High Lobber
skills.definition: A lofted throw used to reach high platforms, towers, or skyward targets. High Lobber builds on Arc Control by maximizing hang time and vertical reach. Often trained in siege, supply drops, or long-range signaling, this skill ensures the throw crests before it crashes. It’s the art of lobbing upward—and making it count.

skills.name: Skip Stone Arc
skills.definition: A technique for sending flat or rounded objects in a low, bouncing trajectory across a surface. Skip Stone Arc is trained for distraction, multipoint impact, or water-based delivery. It combines launch angle, spin, and surface reading into one smooth motion. A throw that dances before it hits.

skills.name: Ricochet Planner
skills.definition: The mastery of bounce and deflection, Ricochet Planner allows throws to hit indirect targets via walls, ceilings, or angled surfaces. This is trick-throw territory—used to bypass shields, blind corners, or create diversions. With enough control, even a miss can become a hit.

skills.name: Stability & Bracing
skills.definition: The core skill of rooting one’s body against shifting forces, impact, or uneven terrain. Stability & Bracing trains the use of posture, limb positioning, and muscle control to hold ground under stress. It enables power to be applied without losing footing or direction. Every motion becomes stronger when it begins from stillness.

skills.name: Recoil Absorption
skills.definition: Recoil Absorption allows the body to endure and redirect force without collapse. It is often used when firing large projectiles, catching heavy impacts, or standing through sudden shifts in pressure. Practitioners are trained to flex at the right joints and angles to dampen force like a living shock absorber. Absorbing doesn’t mean yielding—it means surviving.

skills.name: Shot Kick Down
skills.definition: This skill focuses on the immediate bracing required to neutralize violent backward kick or snap. Shot Kick Down is essential for wielding massive tools, firing siege weapons, or resisting explosive pressure. Users learn how to plant and drop weight mid-action to stop being moved. You don’t fly back—you lock in.

skills.name: Blast Brace
skills.definition: Blast Brace trains the body to anchor against forward or outward explosions of energy or debris. It is a reflexive stance taught to minimize displacement and protect vulnerable joints. The user becomes a wall, not a leaf. In the moment of blast, your footing decides who falls.

skills.name: Force Recoil Mastery
skills.definition: Mastery in the redirection of powerful rebound or blowback into usable counter-force. Force Recoil Mastery turns reaction into weapon—shifting energy from an enemy’s strike into your own return motion. It’s not just resistance—it’s repurposing. True control means nothing is wasted.

skills.name: Counter-Pull
skills.definition: A skill used when opposing forces threaten to drag or yank the user off balance. Counter-Pull trains grip anchoring, core tension, and offset alignment to resist directional pull. Whether holding onto a rope, a beast, or a collapsing platform—this skill keeps you where you chose to stand.

skills.name: Tug Of War Anchor
skills.definition: This skill is the peak of being the last line in a pull-based challenge. Tug Of War Anchor turns the body into a fixed point, capable of holding against sustained force. Used in rescues, tethers, or contests of might, this technique is all about locking down while the world pulls. You don’t budge—you are the anchor.

skills.name: Towline Hold
skills.definition: Designed for long-duration holds under constant directional strain, Towline Hold trains the body to handle progressive fatigue while maintaining grip and stance. It’s often used in transport, climbing, or rescue when constant force threatens to drag the user out of place. You become a living post—the line holds because you do.

skills.name: Tension Anchor
skills.definition: Tension Anchor allows the user to maintain tension across multiple vectors without buckling. Used in crossbeam suspension, live hauling, or group-based load transfers, it enables precise distribution of pull through the whole frame. The strain is real—but so is your control.

skills.name: Reactive Balance
skills.definition: Reactive Balance trains the body to recover equilibrium after slips, shoves, or failed footing. It’s not just about preventing a fall—it’s about reclaiming position mid-motion. Practitioners learn how to drop weight, roll tension, or twist joints into new grounding. You may be moved, but you are never down.

skills.name: Foot Lock Pivot
skills.definition: A technique used to pivot or twist under pressure without giving up base stability. Foot Lock Pivot is essential when shifting angles or redirection while bearing weight. It lets the user rotate with purpose—never dragged, never off center. Movement begins in the feet, and ends with control.

skills.name: Wide Stance Plant
skills.definition: This skill widens the body’s base to resist toppling forces, sway, or imbalance. Wide Stance Plant is used when lifting above the head, holding a flank, or enduring environmental resistance. It turns a person into a platform—one that won’t be moved unless it chooses to move.

skills.name: Dynamic Centering
skills.definition: The refined art of maintaining core alignment while in motion. Dynamic Centering ensures strength flows from the spine and hips no matter the action—lift, strike, pivot, or brace. Practitioners seem immune to stagger or sway. It’s not about standing still—it’s about staying centered even while moving.

skills.name: Grip Mastery
skills.definition: Grip Mastery is the foundation of strength through the hands—training the muscles, tendons, and nerves for unmatched control. It allows for powerful holds, steady lifts, and secure grips in both calm and chaotic conditions. This skill affects every other form of manipulation, from weapons to climbing to restraint. A firm grasp isn’t just strength—it’s presence.

skills.name: Crushing Grip
skills.definition: A specialization in applying overwhelming force through the hands to crush or disable. Crushing Grip is trained for snapping tools, bending bars, or neutralizing limbs. This is brute power focused into the fingers—meant to immobilize, destroy, or dominate through grip alone. A hold that tightens is a hold that ends things.

skills.name: Bone Breaker Hold
skills.definition: The precision use of grip strength to manipulate joints, tendons, and pressure points for pain, damage, or submission. Bone Breaker Hold isn’t a grapple—it’s a verdict. It allows a fighter to disable or control without weapons, relying entirely on anatomical targeting and merciless grip force. One squeeze, and the struggle stops.

skills.name: Two Finger Pinch
skills.definition: A feat of controlled strength and focus, this skill uses minimal contact—often just two fingers—to manipulate, lift, or restrain. Two Finger Pinch is practiced for traps, tools, delicate switches, or subtle pressure applications. Precision doesn’t mean weakness—sometimes less grip delivers more control.

skills.name: Grip Burst
skills.definition: Grip Burst channels sudden explosive tension through the hands in one violent motion. It’s trained for snapping ropes, breaking restraints, or shocking opponents with unexpected strength. Unlike sustained holds, this is one moment of overwhelming force. When it goes off, it breaks things.

skills.name: Endurance Grip
skills.definition: Endurance Grip trains the ability to maintain holds over long durations or extreme strain. Whether hanging, carrying, or climbing—this skill resists fatigue where others would fail. Practitioners develop layered grip control, shifting stress through muscles and joints like a living winch. You don’t drop until it’s over.

skills.name: Rope Hang
skills.definition: This skill specializes in holding one’s full body weight from ropes, vines, or cables for prolonged time. Rope Hang trains shoulder and core assistance to extend grip duration. It’s used in climbing, suspensions, or descents where release means failure. It’s not just about not falling—it’s about never letting go.

skills.name: Cliff Cling
skills.definition: A climbing-based skill used to maintain a grip on irregular, natural, or shifting surfaces. Cliff Cling focuses on finger placement, micro-adjustment, and breath control. It's not about strength alone—but endurance under strain in impossible angles. Practitioners hang where others slide.

skills.name: Static Clamp
skills.definition: Static Clamp enables the body to freeze in position with unyielding grip for balance, restraint, or stealth. It's used to hold positions mid-climb, mid-lift, or mid-fight—without shifting or revealing motion. A single locked position becomes unshakable. This is the stillness that holds everything together.

skills.name: Fine Torque
skills.definition: Fine Torque is the practice of applying subtle rotational force through the hands for mechanical or precision use. It’s used to twist, tighten, or align objects that require accuracy without over-application of strength. When a bolt must seal, a valve must adjust, or a mechanism must move just enough—this is the tool.

skills.name: Valve Twist
skills.definition: Valve Twist specializes in high-resistance rotational force over repetitive motion, especially on stiff or sealed devices. It’s trained for use in ventilation, hydraulics, or large mechanical objects where grip precision meets grunt force. The turn you make might save a life—or flood a chamber.

skills.name: Fine Gear Tensioning
skills.definition: A finesse-driven skill for adjusting the balance between tightness and function in gears, pulleys, or small load systems. Fine Gear Tensioning allows manual fine-tuning under pressure. Too loose, and it slips—too tight, and it breaks. This is where hand strength meets mechanic’s instinct.

skills.name: Tension Locking
skills.definition: This skill locks ropes, belts, clamps, or braces into a fixed position using tension and counterforce. Tension Locking is used in rapid-load scenarios or when a mechanism must hold despite movement or pull. You set it, tighten it, and make damn sure it doesn’t come loose.

skills.name: Agile Movement
skills.definition: Agile Movement is the foundation of responsive, fluid physical motion. It represents training in footwork, body control, and coordination across changing environments. Practitioners move with grace, adjust quickly, and reduce wasted motion. All advanced mobility techniques begin here.

skills.name: Climbing
skills.definition: Climbing develops the body’s ability to ascend or maneuver vertically using arms, legs, and core alignment. It includes scaling natural surfaces, walls, and architectural structures. Practitioners learn grip discipline, route reading, and momentum control. When others stall, you keep rising.

skills.name: Free Ascent
skills.definition: This skill focuses on unassisted climbing—without ropes, ladders, or gear. Free Ascent trains the mind and body to commit fully to vertical movement through calculated handholds and precise weight shifts. It’s raw motion against gravity, no safety net. Those who practice it don’t look down.

skills.name: Overhang Traverse
skills.definition: Overhang Traverse specializes in moving across or beneath suspended surfaces like cave ceilings, ledges, or angled obstacles. It trains lateral weight balance, swing transition, and grip endurance. It’s not enough to climb—you have to hang, shift, and move while suspended.

skills.name: Rapid Descent
skills.definition: The art of moving downward quickly without losing control or safety. Rapid Descent is trained for rope-free drops, angled slides, or climbing down under pressure. It’s not just falling with style—it’s calculated, committed descent with full-body braking.

skills.name: Parkour
skills.definition: Parkour is the refined art of navigating obstacles through continuous motion. It includes vaulting, leaping, balancing, and redirecting momentum in tight or cluttered spaces. Practitioners view terrain as a pathway, not a barrier. This is speed without pause, motion without break.

skills.name: Wall Run
skills.definition: Wall Run is the technique of using speed and foot placement to ascend vertical or angled walls briefly. It blends horizontal momentum with upward force to reach surfaces others can’t. This isn’t fantasy—it’s footwork, friction, and fearlessness.

skills.name: Gap Vault
skills.definition: This skill focuses on transitioning over gaps using arms, legs, and torso in a unified burst. Gap Vault is used to clear fences, ledges, or roof spaces with fluid motion and a clean landing. It’s not about jumping farther—it’s about knowing exactly when and how to leap.

skills.name: Precision Landing
skills.definition: The ability to land from jumps or falls with minimal noise, shock, or loss of balance. Precision Landing trains foot placement, muscle tension release, and momentum bleed. Whether from rooftop or stairwell, the descent ends where you choose.

skills.name: Evasion
skills.definition: Evasion trains the body to avoid incoming danger through reactive movement. It includes ducking, diving, side-steps, rolls, and dodges—all trained under pressure. It’s not about escaping entirely—it’s about not being hit.

skills.name: Combat Roll
skills.definition: Combat Roll enables the user to redirect force and reposition while under direct threat. It’s trained to escape grapples, cross short kill zones, or move under obstacles without exposing vital targets. Where others flinch—you flow.

skills.name: Quick Duck
skills.definition: A reflexive drop of the torso to avoid incoming force or visual detection. Quick Duck is trained to occur within a breath—slipping under blades, limbs, or watchful eyes. Sometimes the best way to win is simply not being where the strike lands.

skills.name: Acrobatic Flip
skills.definition: A dynamic movement technique that turns evasion into opportunity. Acrobatic Flip includes flips, twists, and spinning recoveries used to reposition, disorient, or escape. While flashy, it is also efficient—redirecting threat momentum into unexpected space.

skills.name: Stealth
skills.definition: Stealth is the core practice of remaining unseen, unheard, and unnoticed in hostile or sensitive environments. It encompasses posture, breath, balance, and awareness of surroundings. Practitioners know when to move—and more importantly, when not to. True stealth is presence reduced to shadow.

skills.name: Silent Movement
skills.definition: Silent Movement is the art of traversing space without producing sound. It trains weight distribution, footfall placement, and ambient noise masking. Whether indoors, outdoors, or across unstable terrain, your steps leave no echo. Sound is a footprint—you leave none.

skills.name: Soft Footfall
skills.definition: This skill sharpens the physical discipline of minimizing noise with every step. Soft Footfall is essential for close-quarter infiltration, stalking, or movement on fragile surfaces. It’s not just about going quiet—it’s about being unheard even in total silence.

skills.name: Breath Controll
skills.definition: Controlled Breathing trains respiratory rhythm and tension release to eliminate telltale signs of stress or activity. Used during ambush, waiting, or hiding in plain sight, this skill can slow pulse, suppress noise, and steady the mind. You vanish not just from sight—but from sound itself.

skills.name: Surface Blend
skills.definition: A technique of syncing motion with surface type—carpet, stone, leaves, or sand—so that no step is out of place. Surface Blend teaches foot angle, pace, and pressure unique to each environment. The world becomes your cloak—walk like you belong.

skills.name: Shadowing
skills.definition: Shadowing allows a person to follow or observe another without detection. It trains distance management, cover use, timing, and sightline control. This is not stalking with intent—it’s watching without being seen. To follow well is to disappear just behind the edge of perception.

skills.name: Trail Target
skills.definition: A skill used to maintain consistent proximity to a moving subject without exposing yourself. Trail Target accounts for speed, path prediction, and obstacle negotiation. Practitioners remain close enough to observe—yet always just far enough not to be noticed.

skills.name: Blind Spot Ride
skills.definition: This technique exploits a target’s natural visual and awareness blind spots—rear arcs, shoulder zones, and mental gaps. Blind Spot Ride allows extended tailing even in open or bright environments. It’s not invisibility—it’s perfect placement in human ignorance.

skills.name: Predictive Cut Off
skills.definition: Predictive Cut Off allows a stealth user to move ahead of a target by anticipating their path. It’s used to intercept, ambush, or observe from in front rather than behind. This skill turns passive trailing into silent chess. The target never saw you—yet you’re already there.

skills.name: Concealment
skills.definition: Concealment is the trained act of remaining unseen in open or limited-cover environments. It teaches body placement, environmental usage, and stillness discipline. Whether behind a curtain, within a bush, or beneath a bench, you’re not invisible—but you might as well be.

skills.name: Camouflage
skills.definition: Camouflage enhances concealment by using clothing, materials, or environment blending to break visual recognition. Practiced users can vanish even when technically visible. Shadows, mud, leaves, dust—it all becomes part of the cloak. It’s not trickery—it’s total immersion.

skills.name: Static Hide
skills.definition: This skill teaches how to hold a hidden position for extended periods without fidgeting, revealing breath, or giving away body cues. Static Hide is trained for stakeouts, ambush, or surviving sweeps. The body becomes part of the environment—waiting, watching, and undetected.

skills.name: Environment Meld
skills.definition: Environment Meld combines movement, material use, and posture to dissolve into the scene—trees, rocks, walls, even furniture. It is both instinct and calculation, requiring moment-to-moment adaptation to the space. You don’t hide in the environment. You become it.

skills.name: Melee Weaponry
skills.definition: The foundational skill of handling close-range weapons with precision, fluidity, and awareness. Melee Weaponry includes slashes, thrusts, counters, and footwork coordination. It's not about strength alone—it's about timing, angle, and body control. This is where blades and fists become extensions of the will.

skills.name: Bladed Weapons
skills.definition: A specialization in edged tools—knives, swords, axes, or exotic blades. Bladed Weapons emphasizes cutting lines, recovery angles, and targeting vulnerable points. Practitioners move with grace, minimizing wasted energy and maximizing threat. Each edge becomes a message—sharp, fast, and final.

skills.name: Edge Flow
skills.definition: This skill teaches continuous motion with a blade—linking strikes, blocks, and transitions into an unbroken dance. Edge Flow ensures that the blade never stops moving, forcing opponents to react or retreat. It's not just attacking—it's painting with steel.

skills.name: Precision Thrust
skills.definition: Precision Thrust refines the art of controlled piercing strikes. Used to bypass armor gaps, strike vital zones, or end fights cleanly, this skill is all about point control and depth. The thrust isn’t wild—it’s surgical.

skills.name: Parry Riposte
skills.definition: This skill trains the instant reversal of momentum—deflecting an attack and immediately countering. Parry Riposte requires nerve, timing, and perfect understanding of distance. When used properly, it punishes aggression with elegance.

skills.name: Blunt Weapons
skills.definition: A focus on impact-based weapons like clubs, hammers, and maces. Blunt Weapons favors force redirection, shock generation, and tempo control. It's not about slicing—it's about overwhelming. The user doesn’t cut—they crack.

skills.name: Crushing Swing
skills.definition: Crushing Swing channels full-body force into a downward or sweeping blow. It’s used to break armor, stun enemies, or destroy cover. Practitioners time their swing not just for power—but for rhythm. Each impact lands like punctuation.

skills.name: Momentum Transfer
skills.definition: This skill teaches how to absorb impact and convert it into movement—spinning, repositioning, or chaining into a follow-up strike. Momentum Transfer turns defense into offense and recoil into rhythm. It’s not about stopping—it’s about continuing.

skills.name: Guard Break
skills.definition: Guard Break is the practiced ability to force through an enemy’s defense using well-placed strikes and mechanical leverage. It's used to open up shields, stagger stances, or interrupt blocking rhythms. If the wall won’t move, you make it crumble.

skills.name: Dual Wield
skills.definition: Dual Wield trains coordination between dominant and off-hand weapons. It includes timing, mirrored motion, and weapon balancing. The goal is harmony, not chaos—two blades, one will. True dual wielders don’t alternate—they operate as one.

skills.name: Cross Slash
skills.definition: This technique links weapons in crisscrossing strikes—creating layered pressure and forcing misdirection. Cross Slash is fast, deceptive, and hard to block fully. It’s a rhythm that overwhelms defenses and breaks through timing.

skills.name: Off Hand Block
skills.definition: Off Hand Block uses the secondary weapon defensively—intercepting attacks, creating openings, and resetting rhythm. It reduces dependency on full-body dodges and keeps the user upright in tight engagements. Sometimes it’s not about the weapon—it’s about the window it creates.

skills.name: Flow Transition
skills.definition: Flow Transition is the art of swapping roles between main and off-hand seamlessly during combat. It allows constant unpredictability and opens new strike paths as stance shifts mid-motion. A true practitioner fights as if every angle is home.

skills.name: Precision Ranged
skills.definition: The foundational discipline of targeting with distance weapons using control, timing, and keen awareness. Precision Ranged isn’t about firepower—it’s about placement. It trains hand-eye coordination, breath management, and muscle memory. From bow to blade, the shot lands where you intend it.

skills.name: Bow Mastery
skills.definition: This skill reflects refined handling of bows—short, long, or composite. Bow Mastery covers draw control, stance discipline, and recovery timing. It’s trained for repeated, accurate shots under pressure. The bow becomes silent intent in motion.

skills.name: Quick Nock
skills.definition: Quick Nock focuses on rapid arrow placement and release without sacrificing aim. It’s the difference between one shot and three before the enemy reacts. Practitioners rely on rhythm, grip flow, and draw-line memory to maintain speed without sloppiness.

skills.name: Long Draw
skills.definition: Long Draw trains deep pull and full extension for maximum arrow power and stability. It’s not about raw distance—it’s about tension control and consistent release. The arrow doesn’t fly—it commits.

skills.name: Wind Guage
skills.definition: Wind Guage allows the user to adjust aim dynamically based on wind patterns, terrain drafts, and movement. It’s both environmental awareness and instinctive calculation. A practiced archer doesn’t fight the wind—they use it.

skills.name: Crossbow Mastery
skills.definition: Crossbow Mastery focuses on loading, aiming, and firing precision-based mechanical ranged weapons. It trains brace timing, latch familiarity, and recovery stance. A crossbow doesn’t just fire—it commits with finality.

skills.name: Fast Crank
skills.definition: Fast Crank enables the user to reload or reset the bowstring mechanism quickly—even in pressured environments. It blends tool familiarity with body leverage. The quicker it resets, the quicker you remain in control.

skills.name: Precision Latch
skills.definition: This skill refines the cocking and locking of the string so that release is smooth, silent, and repeatable. Precision Latch eliminates weapon wobble or mechanical error before the shot. Every bolt loosed feels deliberate—like pulling a trigger with no click.

skills.name: Silent Release
skills.definition: Silent Release trains the ability to fire without sound—from the creak of string to the sigh of breath. It is used in ambush, assassination, or animal hunting scenarios. There is no warning. Just a body falling quietly.

skills.name: Thrown Weapons
skills.definition: Thrown Weapons emphasizes accuracy, rotation, and impact force with hand-thrown objects—knives, axes, darts, or custom tools. This skill covers angle judG.O.Dent, spin correction, and release timing. A true practitioner throws not to threaten—but to end things.

skills.name: Spin Control
skills.definition: Spin Control refines how an object rotates mid-air—ensuring tip-first impact and consistent landing. It’s especially vital with knives, spikes, or any weapon affected by trajectory drift. Throwing is easy. Landing true takes mastery.

skills.name: Ricochet Throw
skills.definition: This skill allows a user to bounce thrown weapons off hard surfaces to strike indirect targets or disorient. Ricochet Throw blends arc memory, rebound angle, and force balancing. Walls don’t block your shot—they just give you a second chance to hit.

skills.name: Multiple Throw
skills.definition: Multiple Throw trains the user to release two or more weapons in rapid sequence or simultaneously. Used for crowd control, shock entry, or layered targeting, it’s about overwhelming before they react. One hand throws. The other finishes.

skills.name: Fine Manipulation
skills.definition: The base skill of detailed, controlled hand work—whether for theft, repair, or delicate operation. Fine Manipulation covers steady fingers, touch awareness, and micro-movement discipline. It’s not just nimbleness—it’s mastery over the tiny and precise.

skills.name: Sleight-of-Hand
skills.definition: Sleight-of-Hand trains the ability to misdirect, conceal, or switch objects in plain view. Often used for theft, escape artistry, or stage deception, it relies on fluidity and confidence. What the eye sees is not what the hand holds.

skills.name: Palming
skills.definition: Palming is the art of hiding small objects in the hand without detection. Practiced users can carry coins, tools, or keys undetected—transitioning them in and out of sight seamlessly. It’s not magic—it’s muscle memory and misdirection.

skills.name: Quick Switch
skills.definition: This skill focuses on the ability to replace or exchange one item for another rapidly and undetectably. Quick Switch is used in thievery, sabotage, or infiltration where a moment of confusion can shift power. The move is fast—but the setup is everything.

skills.name: Pick Pockets
skills.definition: Pick Pockets is the classic skill of removing items from a person’s clothing or gear without them noticing. It trains contact softening, body timing, and approach angle. It’s not about luck—it’s practiced pressure and exit speed.

skills.name: Lock Bypass
skills.definition: Lock Bypass trains the use of tension, feel, and tool work to open sealed containers without keys. It emphasizes timing, tactile awareness, and a mental model of lock construction. When done well, the lock never even knows it was opened.

skills.name: Pin Setting
skills.definition: Pin Setting focuses on single-pin manipulation to open complex locks with precision. It’s used when speed must be sacrificed for certainty. The user feels each click and knows when it’s real. This is lockpicking as meditation.

skills.name: Rake Technique
skills.definition: A faster, more aggressive method of picking where multiple pins are disturbed simultaneously. Rake Technique is trained for quick jobs, low-quality locks, or high-pressure breaches. It’s speed over subtlety—a gamble that often pays.

skills.name: Bypass Tooling
skills.definition: This skill allows use of custom tools—shims, bump keys, magnet rigs—to override lock mechanisms creatively. Bypass Tooling expands the possibilities beyond just pins, including latches, tumblers, and levers. It's innovation over tradition.

skills.name: Precision Tinkering
skills.definition: Precision Tinkering trains work on miniature or delicate mechanical systems. From clocks to traps to field repairs, it blends analysis and craftsmanship. Your hands don’t guess—they understand.

skills.name: Micro Gear Work
skills.definition: This skill focuses on adjusting tiny gears and mechanisms by hand or with tools. Used for watches, timers, or intricate devices, Micro Gear Work demands steady nerves and patient rhythm. Each twist could be success—or disaster.

skills.name: Delicate Solder
skills.definition: Delicate Solder is the use of heat, metal, and timing to bind miniature parts without damage. It's used for circuit repair, jewelry, or microscopic enhancements. Too hot, and it warps. Too slow, and it fails. Mastery means perfect melt.

skills.name: Spring Read
skills.definition: A specialized skill for interpreting spring tension, trigger mechanisms, and timing systems within devices. Spring Read allows you to predict when something will snap, pop, or break sequence. It’s the difference between disabling a trap—and setting it off.

skills.name: Vehicle Operation
skills.definition: This is the core skill of physically controlling mounts, vehicles, and mobile platforms. Vehicle Operation includes handling speed, turns, and terrain while maintaining balance and awareness. It's not about driving—it’s about piloting your environment.

skills.name: Mounted Travel
skills.definition: Mounted Travel focuses on controlling animals or rides under motion—horses, beasts, or riding platforms. It trains posture alignment, rhythm sync, and directional command. When you ride, you become part of the motion.

skills.name: Balance Recovery
skills.definition: This skill trains the ability to recover control during a stumble, buck, or sudden directional shift while mounted. Balance Recovery uses body correction, footwork, and grip adjustment to stay on without falling. One misstep doesn’t throw you—you reset and ride on.

skills.name: Rapid Mount/Dismount
skills.definition: Rapid Mount/Dismount allows quick, practiced transitions onto or off of a mount—under pressure, mid-motion, or in combat. It’s about using momentum rather than wasting it. You're in the saddle before the enemy even realizes you moved.

skills.name: Terrain Sprint
skills.definition: Terrain Sprint refines high-speed mounted movement across uneven or shifting ground. It’s trained for dashes across battlefields, ravines, or city rubble. You don’t just charge—you thread the wild.

skills.name: Land Navigation
skills.definition: Land Navigation trains control of land-based vehicles in both predictable and chaotic terrain. It includes steering correction, acceleration judG.O.Dent, and route flow. Whether wagon or wheeled machine, it keeps motion under your hands.

skills.name: Rough Terrain Control
skills.definition: This skill enables the user to maintain vehicle control on jagged, muddy, or obstacle-filled land. Rough Terrain Control includes bounce compensation, wheel angle adaptation, and friction awareness. When the road disappears, you still drive.

skills.name: Drift / Slide Mastery
skills.definition: Drift / Slide Mastery allows the controlled loss and regain of traction for tight turns, sudden stops, or show-of-force movement. It trains throttle balance, weight shift, and tire read. Chaos becomes choreography.

skills.name: Reverse Maneuvering
skills.definition: This skill trains spatial awareness and counter-steering while moving backward or sideways. Reverse Maneuvering is vital in tight spaces, retreats, or repositioning without a full turn. You don’t need to face forward to stay in control.

skills.name: Water & Air Travel
skills.definition: Water & Air Travel teaches basic handling of vessels that float, glide, or fly. It includes current resistance, wind adaptation, and non-ground-based navigation. Movement no longer relies on wheels—it relies on reading the flow.

skills.name: Glide Path Control
skills.definition: Glide Path Control trains the use of lift, descent, and drag to manage air or water trajectory. It’s used for gliders, wingsuits, or lightweight vessels. Smoothness is everything—you don’t drop, you descend.

skills.name: Rapid Launch
skills.definition: This skill enables fast takeoff or push-off from a dock, ledge, or ramp. Rapid Launch is trained to use timing and space without collision or stall. In moments where speed saves lives, you’re already airborne.

skills.name: Gust Correction
skills.definition: Gust Correction refines how a user adjusts trajectory when wind or wave forces act unexpectedly. It involves tension read, correction input, and tilt timing. Storms don’t stop you—they challenge you to stay smooth.

skills.name: Reactive Defense
skills.definition: Reactive Defense is the core skill of avoiding, deflecting, or absorbing attacks in real-time through trained motion. It emphasizes timing, body control, and spatial judG.O.Dent. Practitioners don’t just block—they vanish from danger’s path. This is defense that moves.

skills.name: Dodge Mastery
skills.definition: Dodge Mastery refines the act of narrowly escaping attacks without leaving combat posture. It includes sidesteps, pivots, and slips executed at just the right moment. It’s not about retreat—it’s about repositioning while staying lethal.

skills.name: Evade Burst
skills.definition: This skill allows a sudden, high-speed motion in any direction to escape a strike zone or trap. Evade Burst combines explosive leg power with instinctual timing. One instant, you’re there. The next, you’re gone.

skills.name: Slip Step
skills.definition: A subtle, minimal-movement dodge that shifts the body just enough to avoid impact. Slip Step is trained for tight quarters, multi-attacker engagements, or when retreat isn’t possible. It’s a whisper of motion that changes everything.

skills.name: Split Second Shift
skills.definition: This skill heightens reaction to the extreme—allowing dodges at the very edge of threat contact. Split Second Shift requires trust in instinct and training beyond thought. It’s not a plan—it’s survival triggered by a breath.

skills.name: Deflect / Parry
skills.definition: Deflect / Parry turns incoming strikes aside rather than absorbing or dodging them. It requires timing, angle, and a calm read of motion. This is redirection, not resistance. When done well, it looks like the blow never arrived.

skills.name: Weapon Redirect
skills.definition: A trained technique to guide an attacker’s weapon away from the body using one’s own blade, staff, or tool. Weapon Redirect breaks momentum and control without full blocking. The strike lands—but never where it meant to.

skills.name: Hand Redirect
skills.definition: This skill allows the user to deflect strikes with empty hands—pushing aside blades, fists, or projectiles with fingers, palms, or forearms. Hand Redirect is about flow, not force. You don’t stop the strike—you guide it somewhere else.

skills.name: Momentum Capture
skills.definition: Momentum Capture uses an enemy’s own motion to reposition them—guiding their force into a stumble, roll, or counter. It’s trained to turn attacks into opportunities without striking back. The greater their power, the more they lose control.

skills.name: Roll & Recovery
skills.definition: Roll & Recovery focuses on converting falling, being thrown, or losing balance into motion that resets position. It trains the user to hit the ground in control—and rise again before the enemy adjusts. You don’t fall—you flow.

skills.name: Break Fall
skills.definition: Break Fall teaches how to absorb impact during a fall to avoid injury or loss of momentum. It’s used in combat, travel, or acrobatics where landing wrong ends things fast. Hit the ground—but never break on it.

skills.name: Tumbling Egress
skills.definition: This skill enables the user to roll out of grapples, tight spaces, or chaotic zones without exposing vulnerable areas. Tumbling Egress is trained for fluid escapes and fast re-engagement. You leave danger like wind leaves flame—fast, low, silent.

skills.name: Spring Up
skills.definition: Spring Up allows a character to rise from prone or fallen position instantly—often mid-motion. It’s a blend of timing, spine alignment, and muscle sync. You don’t get back up—you launch up.

skills.name: Aerial Operation
skills.definition: Aerial Operation is the discipline of navigating, maneuvering, and maintaining control while suspended or moving above ground. It includes jumping, swinging, gliding, falling, and landing safely or tactically. This isn’t just motion—it’s gravity turned into a tool.

skills.name: Aerial Balance
skills.definition: Aerial Balance trains the body to remain upright, stable, and reactive while in the air or on narrow, unstable footing. It includes micro-adjustments, posture shifts, and wind response. The sky doesn’t forgive imbalance—you become your own anchor.

skills.name: Ledge Cling Transition
skills.definition: This skill allows for hanging from a ledge or grip point and transitioning fluidly to climb, swing, or drop. It’s trained for both stealth and emergency movement. You're not just holding on—you’re choosing when and how to move.

skills.name: Rope Tension Walk
skills.definition: Rope Tension Walk refines movement across tensioned lines—ropes, cables, or narrow beams. It trains center alignment, pace, and wind adjustment. You don’t balance like a dancer—you balance like your life depends on it.

skills.name: Wind Balance Recovery
skills.definition: Wind Balance Recovery is the reflexive ability to regain control mid-air or on platforms buffeted by gusts or turbulence. It’s used on airships, cliffs, or during long jumps. The wind pushes—you respond without panic, without pause.

skills.name: Lift and Drop
skills.definition: Lift and Drop is the study of vertical movement—climbing, dropping, hoisting, or rappelling. It trains rope use, friction control, and jump-drop technique. This is not falling—it’s descending with intention.

skills.name: Anchor Descent
skills.definition: Anchor Descent focuses on controlled rope or tether-based lowering, especially from dangerous heights. It includes grip cycling, angle awareness, and surface response. You don’t let go of the rope—the rope lets you down when you decide.

skills.name: Midair Brake
skills.definition: This skill allows the user to slow or redirect motion while airborne using limbs, body tension, or tools. Midair Brake is used to hit smaller targets, avoid collisions, or control descent speed. The fall may be fast, but you land like it never was.

skills.name: Controlled Landing
skills.definition: Controlled Landing refines how one hits the ground—absorbing force, minimizing injury, and staying ready. It’s used from drops, leaps, or even forced throws. You don’t crash—you arrive.

skills.name: Free Movement
skills.definition: Free Movement trains mid-air directional control—swinging, leaping, twisting, or shifting during jumps or glides. It’s motion within motion. Gravity doesn’t own you—you negotiate with it.

skills.name: Swing Arc Control
skills.definition: This skill teaches how to manipulate swing arcs for length, height, and timing. It’s trained on ropes, bars, vines, or hooks. Practitioners can redirect, increase, or kill momentum at will. When you swing, it’s not just to move—it’s to position.

skills.name: Leap Redirection
skills.definition: Leap Redirection is the skill of changing trajectory mid-jump using body twist, terrain contact, or obstacles. It’s used to evade mid-air, reach platforms, or fake direction. You launch once—but you land where they didn’t expect.

skills.name: Glider Pivot
skills.definition: This technique controls the shift of direction, tilt, or roll in gliding devices like wingsuits, magical cloaks, or paragliders. It trains airflow reading and body torque. You don’t drift in the air—you steer it.

skills.name: Rigging & Ropework
skills.definition: The foundation of working with ropes, cords, and suspension systems. Rigging & Ropework covers tying, securing, and anchoring through technique and tension. It’s more than knots—it’s control over movement, load, and survival.

skills.name: Knot Mastery
skills.definition: Knot Mastery refines the tying of secure, purpose-driven knots under pressure or in motion. From harnesses to bindings, each knot has intent. Practitioners tie with speed, accuracy, and muscle memory.

skills.name: Rapid Tie
skills.definition: This skill enables fast creation of strong knots using minimal movement. It’s trained for rescue, capture, or setup in urgent situations. Your hands tie faster than most can react.

skills.name: Escape Knot
skills.definition: Escape Knot teaches how to tie or recognize knots that can be undone from tension or specific motion. It’s used for stealth, survival, or illusion. Bound doesn’t mean trapped.

skills.name: Hidden Knot
skills.definition: This technique conceals knots within fabric folds, shadows, or other bindings. Hidden Knot makes restraints, traps, or tricks harder to detect or disable. A knot unseen is a knot unbeaten.

skills.name: Rappelling
skills.definition: Rappelling focuses on controlled descent using rope, anchors, and body positioning. It’s trained for cliffs, towers, and vertical access scenarios. Every drop is calculated, every grip tested.

skills.name: Fast Descent
skills.definition: This skill enables rapid downward movement while maintaining safety through friction control and footwork. Often used in breaches, chases, or collapses. The line becomes your lifeline—and your weapon.

skills.name: Wall Transition
skills.definition: Wall Transition teaches how to switch between climbing, wall contact, and rope use mid-action. It blends climbing and descent into a seamless sequence. You’re never between options—you are the transition.

skills.name: Underhang Swing
skills.definition: Underhang Swing allows the user to navigate underneath ledges or bridges using rope control and pendulum movement. Trained for access and escape in vertical structures. The drop is dangerous—but it leads to freedom.

skills.name: Load Rigging
skills.definition: Load Rigging trains securing and lifting heavy objects with balanced tension and anchor setups. Used in hauling, transport, or building situations. It’s not just weight—it’s motion made safe.

skills.name: Balanced Suspension
skills.definition: This skill distributes weight evenly across multiple points to avoid tipping or strain. It’s vital for moving bodies, equipment, or sensitive containers. Balance is what keeps it alive.

skills.name: Quick Release
skills.definition: Quick Release trains rigging techniques that allow for instant rope drop or disconnect. It’s used in combat zones, escape plans, or precision deliveries. When time matters, one pull resets the field.

skills.name: Highline Setup
skills.definition: Highline Setup allows the user to rig a suspended rope bridge, zipline, or elevated path across gaps. Used for travel, tension lines, or suspended traversal. The void between points becomes a path—because you build one.

skills.name: Precision Performance
skills.definition: A display of mastery in coordinated, fine-tuned hand and body movements for spectacle, training, or mental control. Precision Performance includes displays like juggling, card work, and object manipulation. It's not about flash—it's about focus.

skills.name: Juggling
skills.definition: Juggling trains rhythm, spatial awareness, and coordination with multiple airborne objects. It's used for entertainment, training reflexes, or distraction. Each toss is a lesson in timing.

skills.name: Multi Object
skills.definition: This skill pushes the limit of how many items can be juggled while maintaining a clean pattern. Multi Object requires balance, breathing, and serious nerve. The chaos becomes choreography.

skills.name: Bounce Juggle
skills.definition: Bounce Juggle manipulates object rebound off surfaces—walls, floors, shields—within a juggling flow. Trained to redirect motion with force and precision. Gravity becomes part of the rhythm.

skills.name: Fire Juggle
skills.definition: Fire Juggle adds live flame to juggling sequences, requiring perfect catch alignment and burn control. It’s trained for performance—or distraction tactics in extreme scenarios. One slip, and it’s pain. Master it, and it’s mesmerizing.

skills.name: Cardistry
skills.definition: Cardistry is the refined manipulation of cards for artistic display, distraction, or subtle movement. It’s not about tricks—it’s about elegance. The cards don’t flutter—they obey.

skills.name: One Hand Cut
skills.definition: This skill allows full deck shuffles, cuts, or reversals using only one hand. Often used for flair, distraction, or sleight setups. It’s elegance through constraint.

skills.name: Flourish Fan
skills.definition: Flourish Fan trains the graceful spread of cards into arcs or fans, showing smoothness and control. Often used to create presence or open a scene. Control that looks like art.

skills.name: Throw Catch
skills.definition: Throw Catch allows controlled projection and retrieval of cards mid-air. Used for show or sleight setups. When you throw, they think it's gone—until it snaps back.

skills.name: Dexterous Display
skills.definition: Dexterous Display includes advanced hand feats that impress, confuse, or test skill—coins, loops, knots, and more. It’s coordination put on display. You don’t just move—you demonstrate.

skills.name: Coin Roll Cascade
skills.definition: This skill rolls coins continuously across fingers, wrists, or surfaces without break. A signature of stillness within motion. You don’t drop—it flows.

skills.name: One-Hand Knot Loop
skills.definition: One-Hand Knot Loop trains fast knot formation using only one hand—blindfolded, distracted, or mid-action. For show, escape, or intimidation. They blink—and the knot is done.

skills.name: Thread The Needle Toss
skills.definition: A high-precision throw through narrow or moving gaps—showcasing focus and judG.O.Dent. Used for performance or challenge. You aim where no one dares—and hit exactly there.

skills.name: Endurance
skills.definition: The foundation of grit and stamina, Endurance measures a character's ability to press forward through pain, exhaustion, and strain. Whether climbing peaks, surviving brutal marches, or outlasting hunger, this skill reflects an inner reserve that refuses to break. It is the quiet strength behind every step taken when others would fall.

skills.name: Distance Running
skills.definition: The art of sustained movement over long distances, Distance Running transforms effort into rhythm. Those trained in this skill find a steady pace that seems to defy fatigue, crossing terrain with a tempered, tireless stride. It's not about speed—it's about never stopping.

skills.name: Marathoner
skills.definition: Pushing the body beyond its limits, the Marathoner becomes a creature of pure will. They eat miles for breakfast, wear down time itself, and run through storms without flinching. Where others collapse, they only find another gear.

skills.name: Pace Control
skills.definition: Knowing when to surge and when to conserve, Pace Control is the strategy behind endurance. With this mastery, every breath, heartbeat, and footfall is measured. The runner becomes their own metronome, never wasting energy.

skills.name: Hill Sprint Recovery
skills.definition: The lungs burn, the slope climbs, but recovery comes fast and clean. This skill reflects the body’s rapid rebound after bursts of punishing effort. The ground may rise sharply, but so does the will to keep going.

skills.name: Pack Trekking
skills.definition: Built for burdened movement across long journeys, Pack Trekking turns travel into triumph. Whether hauling supplies through forest or canyon, this skill transforms weight from a hindrance into momentum. It’s how the strong carry the world.

skills.name: Weighted Trek
skills.definition: With load comes discipline. Weighted Trek represents the ability to bear mass over distance without loss of speed or form. The trekker moves with quiet resolve, each step honed through training under strain.

skills.name: Terrain Traverse
skills.definition: From desert dunes to mountain paths, this skill allows the body to adapt its gait, rhythm, and resilience to unforgiving ground. Uneven terrain becomes challenge, not obstacle. The path doesn’t change—it’s the traveler who does.

skills.name: Endurance Shuffle
skills.definition: The movement isn't fast, but it never stops. This skill channels tired muscles into low-effort motion, allowing progress even when energy is nearly spent. It’s how you cross the last miles when strength alone won't carry you.

skills.name: Cardio Conditioning
skills.definition: Cardio Conditioning builds the heart into a weapon of efficiency. Breath deepens, circulation sharpens, and effort becomes sustainable over time. This is the engine beneath the endurance.

skills.name: VO₂ Max Boost
skills.definition: A reflection of peak internal function, this skill enhances the body's oxygen use to near superhuman levels. More air, more power, less fatigue—fueling performance where others gasp.

skills.name: Fatigue Resistance
skills.definition: This is where the body shrugs off exhaustion like rain off armor. Trained to delay the onset of physical fatigue, this skill allows activity to stretch far beyond normal bounds. Tired is no longer a limit.

skills.name: Burst Recovery
skills.definition: Between the sprints, the body rebounds with unnatural speed. This skill grants rapid return from short, intense effort—ideal for those who fight in flurries, then move again. It's the calm between storms.

skills.name: Swimming
skills.definition: A full-body expression of rhythm and breath, Swimming turns the water from a barrier into a medium of motion. It measures your ability to stay afloat, move efficiently, and endure the physical demands of aquatic travel. Whether calm or churning, the water yields to trained effort.

skills.name: Speed Swim
skills.definition: Cutting through water with streamlined precision, Speed Swim emphasizes short bursts of speed and efficiency. Every stroke becomes a blade, every kick a propellant. This is swimming with intent and urgency.

skills.name: Sprint Freestyle
skills.definition: Designed for explosive velocity, Sprint Freestyle unleashes a torrent of speed over short distances. This is where technique, timing, and raw energy combine to dominate the lane—or the chase.

skills.name: Flip Turn Mastery
skills.definition: A signature move of elite swimmers, the flip turn transforms wall contact into momentum. With perfect timing and minimal energy loss, the swimmer becomes a constant flow—never stopping, only redirecting.

skills.name: Explosive Start
skills.definition: The dive is a launch, not a leap. Explosive Start captures the burst of muscular force and breath control needed to launch from the edge with precision and impact. The race begins before you hit the water.

skills.name: Endurance Swim
skills.definition: This is the slow, steady rhythm that turns oceans into corridors and lakes into lanes. Endurance Swim teaches the body to maintain motion over long distances, conserving energy while resisting fatigue.

skills.name: Marathon Swim
skills.definition: Whether across a bay or down a river, the Marathon Swim is about sustained determination. Hours in the water become a trance of motion, where the body adapts to cold, current, and exhaustion.

skills.name: Open Water Navigator
skills.definition: In unfamiliar waters, this skill grants instinctual guidance. Through feel, landmarks, and body awareness, the swimmer holds a straight path despite waves, wind, or disorientation.

skills.name: Current Negotiation
skills.definition: The water has its own will—and this skill turns it into an ally. Trained swimmers sense shifts in flow and adjust in real-time, reducing drag and letting the current do part of the work.

skills.name: Breath Management
skills.definition: The difference between comfort and panic in the water, Breath Management trains control under pressure. Each inhale is intentional; each exhale calculated. This is survival through rhythm.

skills.name: Breath Hold
skills.definition: Whether diving deep or waiting for the moment to surface, Breath Hold extends the body’s limits. It transforms gasping urgency into calm focus—controlling the mind as much as the lungs.

skills.name: Underwater Glide
skills.definition: A fusion of breath control and body position, this skill lets the swimmer travel farther underwater with minimal effort. Like a shadow beneath the waves, they conserve energy and avoid detection.

skills.name: SWB Prevention
skills.definition: Shortness of breath is dangerous; shallow water blackout is fatal. This skill reflects trained instincts to detect signs of overextension and surface before blackout. It’s how discipline prevents disaster.

skills.name: Disease & Toxin Resistance
skills.definition: This foundational skill represents the body’s natural resilience to illness and internal threats. From foreign pathogens to environmental toxins, it measures how well your system endures and neutralizes invasive forces. It’s not just survival—it’s built-in defense.

skills.name: Pathogen Immunity
skills.definition: Through exposure, training, or genetic strength, the immune system becomes a fortress. Pathogen Immunity reflects an enhanced resistance to biological threats, including viruses, bacteria, and other disease-bearing agents. It's preparation before infection strikes.

skills.name: Viral Barrier
skills.definition: This skill strengthens the body's frontline against viral invasion. Whether airborne or bloodborne, viruses find their progress slowed, weakened, or stopped entirely. Infections struggle to take root.

skills.name: Bacterial Shield
skills.definition: Representing an advanced microbiome and immune coordination, this skill repels bacterial spread. Cuts resist festering, contaminated food is more easily endured, and infection fails to gain ground.

skills.name: Rapid Fever Response
skills.definition: Fever becomes a tactical response, not a weakness. This skill trains the body to launch fast, effective thermal defenses against sickness, minimizing downtime and damage from systemic infection.

skills.name: Poison Neutralisation
skills.definition: Where others fall from tainted meals or venomous wounds, this skill reflects the body’s ability to isolate and break down harmful compounds. The reaction is immediate, the defense internal.

skills.name: Venom Metaboliser
skills.definition: The body identifies and breaks down venom at the molecular level, minimizing paralysis, pain, or fatal damage. It’s not immunity—it’s rapid adaptation. The serpent's bite loses its sting.

skills.name: Alkaloid Flush
skills.definition: From toxic plants to ingested poisons, this skill trains the system to purge alkaloids quickly. Nausea fades, clarity returns, and the gut reasserts control before harm spreads.

skills.name: Heavy Metal Chelation
skills.definition: When exposed to dangerous metals—mercury, lead, arsenic—this skill triggers natural chelation responses. The body binds and expels what would otherwise accumulate and poison from within.

skills.name: Biochemical Purge
skills.definition: The body's detox systems work in harmony, flushing out residual toxins and restoring homeostasis. It’s not just resistance—it’s internal cleanliness maintained under stress.

skills.name: Detox Sweat
skills.definition: Through skin and salt, the body eliminates toxins efficiently. This skill increases sweat-based purification, reducing internal buildup and supporting immune and organ functions.

skills.name: Hepatic Boost
skills.definition: The liver becomes a biofilter of unmatched potency. This skill enhances its function, improving toxin breakdown, metabolic regulation, and cellular resilience against overload.

skills.name: Renal Clearance
skills.definition: The kidneys become relentless purifiers, rapidly cycling waste and filtering out contaminants. This skill ensures clarity, hydration balance, and resistance to bloodborne toxicity.

skills.name: Environmental Survival
skills.definition: This skill represents the body’s capacity to endure and adapt to hostile environments. From extreme temperatures to low oxygen and airborne hazards, it’s the cornerstone of survival in unforgiving terrain. Whether on icy peaks or toxic ruins, this is the strength to persist.

skills.name: Thermal Tolerance
skills.definition: The body trains itself to withstand extremes of heat and cold. Thermal Tolerance reduces the risk of shock or exhaustion, keeping vital systems stable even as the climate turns brutal.

skills.name: Heat Resistance
skills.definition: This skill refines your body's ability to withstand high temperatures. Whether under desert suns or near open flame, your system stays cool, alert, and functional.

skills.name: Cold Resistance
skills.definition: The chill no longer bites as deeply. Cold Resistance reflects the body’s improved circulation and resilience in freezing conditions—frostbite and hypothermia slowed or prevented entirely.

skills.name: Rapid Rewarm
skills.definition: When cold strikes fast, this skill allows the body to recover core temperature with speed and safety. Shivering becomes efficient heat, and hypothermia is driven off before it settles in.

skills.name: Altitude Adaptation
skills.definition: Above the clouds, air thins and pressure drops—but this skill lets the body adjust. It reflects gradual changes in breathing, circulation, and internal balance at high elevation.

skills.name: Hypoxia Control
skills.definition: This skill teaches the body to survive on less oxygen. Breathing deep, blood enriched, the character can stay active longer at altitude with less fatigue or confusion.

skills.name: Fast Acclimation
skills.definition: Where others need days or weeks, this body adapts in hours. Fast Acclimation allows a rapid response to elevation change, minimizing sickness and maximizing performance.

skills.name: Thin Air Efficiency
skills.definition: At high elevations, every breath counts. This skill reflects optimized lung and blood function, allowing endurance and clarity where others gasp for air.

skills.name: Atmospheric Endurance
skills.definition: This skill bolsters internal resilience against polluted, low-quality, or unstable air. It represents adaptation to smog, decay, or post-apocalyptic ruin without losing function.

skills.name: Gas Filtration
skills.definition: The lungs act as a more efficient filter, reducing intake of harmful particles or gases. This skill delays onset of nausea, disorientation, or collapse from airborne threats.

skills.name: Smoke Resistance
skills.definition: Whether in burning buildings or choking wastelands, this skill enables functioning amid smoke. The body learns to pace breath, resist coughing, and remain alert through haze.

skills.name: Toxic Inhalation Mitigation
skills.definition: When surrounded by poisonous air, this skill buys critical time. Through trained breath control and immune responses, the worst effects of airborne toxins are delayed or reduced.

skills.name: Pain Management
skills.definition: Pain is inevitable—suffering is optional. This foundational skill trains the body and mind to handle physical trauma without losing control, clarity, or composure. It is the art of staying present through agony.

skills.name: Shock Control
skills.definition: When the body is hit hard enough to shut down, this skill keeps it online. Shock Control represents internal stabilization—keeping heart rate, blood flow, and awareness intact during trauma.

skills.name: Pain Mitigation
skills.definition: This advanced technique allows the mind to override incoming pain signals. Like a gate slammed shut, overwhelming stimuli are delayed or blocked entirely, granting precious moments of clarity.

skills.name: Trauma Dissociation
skills.definition: The self separates from the suffering. Trauma Dissociation is the ability to mentally compartmentalize injury, allowing survival and action in situations where pain would otherwise cripple.

skills.name: Nerve Dampening
skills.definition: This skill reduces the intensity of pain through trained neurological control. Sensations are dulled without losing awareness—allowing wounds to exist without owning the moment.

skills.name: Focus Through Pain
